images.caption: Figure 1:Four paradigms for chain-of-thought reasoning.Explicit CoT:discretetext tokens.Coconut:deterministichidden states.LaDiR:iterativelydenoised latents using diffusion.NF-CoT (ours): AR-sampled continuous thoughts.

Figure 2:Training and inference pipeline of NF-CoT.Training uses a frozen VAE encoder and shallow flow blocks to convert
discrete-CoT rationales into continuous-CoT statesu1:ku_{1:k}. A shared LLM
then consumes the prompt,<BOT>, continuous-CoT states, and
ground-truth text tokensx1:nx_{1:n}in one causal pass, jointly optimizing
the flow NLL and answer cross-entropy. At inference, the VAE branch and
shallow blocks are skipped: the LLM samples predicted continuous-CoT statesu~1:k\tilde{u}_{1:k}left-to-right and continues decoding predicted text
tokensx^1:n^\hat{x}_{1:\hat{n}}with the same KV cache, without recomputing the prompt or CoT prefix.

Figure 3:Pass@kkscaling on MBPP+ and HumanEval+. NF-CoT outperforms the
base model and LaDiR, and continues to improve with largerkk.

Figure 4:Pass@k diversity before and after reinforcement learning on MBPP+ and HumanEval+.
Top row: standard token-space GRPO improves the low-kkregion but saturates at largerkk,
failing to provide the same large-sample coverage as the base model. Bottom row: latent-space
RL for NF-CoT improves pass@1 while preserving the upward pass@k scaling trend, indicating
that policy-gradient refinement in continuous-CoT space does not collapse the latent trajectory
distribution.

Figure 5:Effect of continuous-CoT perturbation strength on HumanEval generation. Asσ\sigmaincreases, the perturbed continuous-CoT trajectory becomes nearly orthogonal to the original trajectory, and exact-text match drops sharply. Nevertheless, pass@1 remains nearly flat, indicating that local perturbations change the form of the generated program much more than its functional correctness, even at the largest perturbation strengths tested.

header: Appendix AQualitative Analysis of Decoded Latent CoTs

images.caption:
Figure 6:Pairwise output similarity among passing HumanEval programs. Each panel
contains32=4×832=4\times 8passing samples from four HumanEval problems,
with intra-task blocks on the diagonal and cross-task entries set to
zero to isolate within-prompt structure. Similarity is computed using a
structure-aware AST metric; lower values indicate higher diversity.
NF-CoT achieves lower mean intra-prompt similarity than the
Qwen3-8B-Base teacher, indicating more structurally diverse passing
solutions under the same answer temperature.
